item.title: Pathological Periodicity, Part I: Introduction and Preliminaries
item.dc:creator: Alonso Espinosa Domínguez
item.description:
1. Introduction

It is an elementary exercise in linear algebra to show that the set of all periodic functions is not a vector subspace of , the space of all real-valued functions on . This is because the sum of periodic functions with incommensurable periods (i.e., periods whose ratio is irrational) often fails to be periodic. However, this leads to many more subtle questions. For instance:

  • Do there exist any periodic functions with incommensurable periods whose sum is nonetheless periodic? Can we characterize such functions?
  • Constant functions are trivially periodic with any real period. Does there exist a non-constant periodic function with 2 or more incommensurable periods? If so, does there exist such a function that is not constant almost everywhere?
  • Which subsets of periodic functions are vector spaces? Can any such vector space contain two or more functions with incommensurable periods?
  • How do the answers to these questions change when considering various families of periodic functions, such as continuous, strictly periodic, measurable functions, etc.?

As it turns out, these seemingly simple questions open a can of worms involving many fields of mathematics at once: namely algebra, measure theory, topology, and the some set theory. In particular, by exploiting the nature of as both a -module and -vector space, we can construct some pathological examples answering most of the above questions in the affirmative. And if we use the Axiom of Choice (AC) on quotients , where is either a -submodule of or a -subspace of , we can answer all the questions in the affirmative (at the cost of sacrificing measurability). This is all very closely related to what is called the Periodic Decomposition Problem, about which much has been written (see for instance this paper).

I am working now on figuring out 2 main questions: first, though it is clear that alone is not enough to show the existence of the nonmeasurable examples mentioned, I’d like to know how many can be found in systems strictly weaker than full ZFC, or if their existence is actually equivalent to AC (I suspect not). Second, I’d like to know just how large the dimension of a vector space of periodic functions can be when it contains functions with incommensurable periods (I suspect not very large). While I work on these questions (and write up the results more formally), I want to use this Pathological Periodicity series in the blog to further motivate this line of inquiry and hopefully inspire others to explore this and related topics further.

2. Preliminaries

Let’s review some basic definitions and facts about periodic functions that set the stage for our further explorations:

Definition 2.1 (Periodic Function) A function is said to be periodic if there exists a non-zero real number such that for all . If there exists a smallest positive such , it is called the fundamental period of , and we say is strictly periodic.

Remark: Note that a function can be periodic without being strictly periodic; for instance, the constant functions are periodic with any real period, but they are not strictly periodic. In this series we will examine pathological examples of functions that fail to be strictly periodic but are also not constant. Some are even constant only on sets of measure zero, which is as far from being constant as one can get. We will also see pathological functions that are actually strictly periodic.

Definition 2.2 (The Period Module) For any function , we denote by the set of all periods of , i.e., . We call the period module of , for reasons that will become clear in a moment. Also, we say are incommensurable if , and they are commensurable otherwise. Note constant functions are trivially commensurable with any periodic function, since they are periodic with any period. And note that non-periodic functions still have a period module, but it is just .

Definition 2.3 (-submodule and its dimension) A subset of is called a -submodule if it is closed under addition and under multiplication by integers. The dimension of a -submodule is the cardinality of a maximal linearly independent subset of over .

Definition 2.4 (-subspace and Period Space) For any function , we can consider the linear span of its period module over the rational numbers . We denote this by , and call it the period space of . By definition, is a -subspace of , which is how we denote the vector space of over .

We immediately find some basic facts about periodic functions and about the algebra and topology of their period modules and spaces:

  • Fact 0: If are incommensurable functions, any (nonzero) periods must be incommensurable real numbers. This is because if and are such that , then there exist integers such that , and thus , showing that and are not incommensurable functions.
  • Fact 1: If is periodic with period , then it is also periodic with respect to any integer linear combinations of periods of . This means is a -submodule of .
  • Fact 2: If is strictly periodic with fundamental period , then the set of all periods of is precisely the -module . I.e., is necessarily a one-dimensional submodule and is a one-dimensional -subspace.
  • Fact 3: The dimension of the period module as a -module is not necessarily the same as the dimension of the period space as a -vector space. For instance, if , its dimension as a -module is infinite, but its dimension as a -subspace () is exactly 1.
  • Fact 4: If is dense, then is either constant or else it is nowhere continuous. To see this, suppose is not constant and let . For any open interval containing , the density of guarantees the existence of periods such that . Since is a -submodule, the difference is a strictly positive period. The closed interval has length exactly , meaning it spans a full period of . Consequently, the image of under is the entire range of . Because is not constant, its range contains some value distinct from , guaranteeing there is some such that . Thus, cannot be continuous at .

We can start to see how closely the nature of a periodic function relates to the topological and algebraic features of its period module. The following propositions make this even clearer:

Proposition 2.6 is dense if and only if is not strictly periodic. Thus, if a periodic is non-constant and continuous, it must be strictly periodic.

Proof: If is dense, then the infimum of positive periods must be zero, and thus no fundamental period can exist. If no fundamental period exists, the infimum must be zero for otherwise we could find two distinct periods arbitrarily close to , and their difference would be a period smaller than , violating the infimum. Thus any interval around must contain elements of . Now take any interval with . By the foregoing, we can find with There thus must be some integer so that , for otherwise we could find an so that . But showing is dense in . The last part is an immediate consequence of the above and of Fact 4.

Proposition 2.7: If are incommensurable (), then the -module is dense in . In particular, if a function has two incommensurable periods and , then , meaning its period module is dense and is not strictly periodic. Therefore non-constant functions with incommensurable periods are nowhere continuous.

Proof: To see the first part of the proposition, we make use of the fact that irrationals can be approximated by rationals very nicely: by Dirichlet’s approximation theorem, we know that for any , we can find integers such that , and thus . This shows all intervals containing the origin have elements of . From here density of follows in the same way as density of in the previous proposition. The second part of the proposition then follows immediately from everything dicussed previously.

3. The difference operator and periodic sums

Now, we revisit the quesiton of sums of incommensurable functions, and in particular we show that in the continous case, such sums are never periodic.

For this and for the future posts in this series, we introduce the following operator which is ubiquitously used in discussions of the periodic deconposition problem:

Definition 3.1 (Difference Operator) For any function and any , we define the difference operator in p by for all . As the reader can verify, these are linear operators for all , and they commute with each other, i.e., for all .

This operator measures how much changes when we translate by . In particular, is identically zero if and only if is a period of . A few more key facts about difference operators and sums of functions:

Facts about Difference Operators:

  • For any and any , we have . That is, all periods of are also periods of .
  • Given two functions , their sum is periodic if and only if there is an such that Such an is then a period of .
  • Thus, if is periodic, then for all and all we have which implies
  • Hence, if is periodic, then and are commensurable for all and all , even if and are incommensurable. This however is not a sufficient condition for to be periodic as the reader can verify (we will also see this in the next installement).
  • When are incommensurable and their sum is periodic, then the above must be incommensurable with all periods of and , and so we actually have
  • If are themsleves periodic and incommensurable with periodic, the above then implies and must have dense period modules and thus be either constant or nowhere continous.

This leads us to the final result of this installment

Proposition 3.2: Let be continuous periodic functions. Then is periodic if and only if are commensurable. Thus no set of continuous periodic functions containing two or more incommensurable functions can be a vector subspace of

We leave the proof to the reader (hint: apply the final fact listed about difference operators and use the boundedness of continuous periodic functions).

Beyond Continuity: A Preview of Pathology

With what we have established so far, a clear structural dichotomy emerges: any periodic function with a dense (and thus higher-dimensional) period module, and any pair of incommensurable functions whose sum manages to remain periodic, must be pathological—specifically, they must be discontinuous everywhere.

In the next installment of Pathological Periodicity, we will dive directly into the abyss of these nowhere-continuous functions. We will construct and dissect a variety of pathological examples, including some highly unintuitive functions that are constant only on sets of Lebesgue measure zero—a construction where the Axiom of Choice becomes absolutely indispensable. Stay tuned!

item.title: Welcome to Mathematical Adventures!
item.dc:creator: Alonso Espinosa Domínguez
item.description:
Welcome to Mathematical Adventures! After many years away from mathematics, I am finally making my way back to this beautiful subject. In this blog, I will first and foremost share my reflections on mathematical topics as I dive into exploring (and re-learning) them. I hope to also provide some meta-mathematical reflections touching on philosophy, pedagogy, and the travails of becoming a mathematician in the current social, political, and economic context.

When it comes to the mathematical content of this blog, I hope to step away from the highly polished, often opaque final forms of classical theorems and actually get our hands dirty. We will explore rigorous mathematics with the mindset of an adventurer: taking the scenic routes, avoiding black-box machinery whenever possible, and building our intuition from the ground up. I think this approach is not only more fun, but also more rewarding in terms of deep understanding, especially for anyone like myself needing to re-learn the basics.

This blog will contain some stand alone posts, and some series. The first series I am launching, Pathological Periodicity, explores the structure of the sets of periods of periodic functions the pathologies that arise within the set of periodic functions whenever we assume the axiom of choice, and related matters. Hop on over to the first post to get started!
